content.text: A launch retrospective for an AI product is not a generic engineering retrospective with "AI" in the title. Generative AI features surface a distinct class of surprises — model behavior that diverged from eval results, prompts that broke on real user phrasing, tools that were unreliable at production scale, and latency-cost tradeoffs that looked different in production than in load tests. An effective AI launch retrospective captures these surprises, converts them into
categorized, owned action items
, and closes the loop on them through the sprint system — not through a shared doc that nobody reads after the meeting.
What a Launch Retrospective Is

A launch retrospective is a structured meeting held within one week of a major release, where the team reviews what happened during the launch period (typically the two weeks before and four weeks after launch), categorizes observations into actionable themes, and assigns ownership to concrete improvements. The output is a set of
action items with DRIs and deadlines
— not a vague list of "things to improve" that lives in a Confluence page.
Why This Matters
AI launches surface novel failure modes:
hallucination edge cases, tool call failures, and prompt brittleness do not appear in conventional retrospective formats. Teams that use generic retro templates miss these categories entirely.
Without closure tracking, retro debt compounds:

teams that run retros but never close the action items generate a growing backlog of known problems. The next retro's opening five minutes should always be reviewing whether last retro's items were resolved.
Retrospective insights drive model improvements:
the most valuable input for prompt iteration, eval suite expansion, and fine-tuning decisions often comes from the structured analysis of launch surprises — not from metrics dashboards.
Retrospective Structure

attrs.alt: AI launch retrospective structure: Pre-retro data → Meeting (timeline + AI categories + action drafting) → Taxonomy → Follow-up loop

content.text: Pre-Retro Data Collection
The facilitator collects the following inputs at least 24 hours before the meeting and distributes them as pre-reading. The goal is to ground the discussion in facts, not in whoever speaks first.
Production metrics snapshot:
error rates, latency percentiles, uptime, support ticket volume, and CSAT for the launch window vs. the 30-day pre-launch baseline.
Incident log:
every incident (P0–P2) that occurred during the launch window, with severity, duration, and resolution summary.
Model behavior surprises log:
a list of prompt inputs and model outputs that surprised the team — cases where the model refused unexpectedly, hallucinated, or produced off-topic responses that were not caught in eval.
Cost vs. forecast:
actual token spend and model API cost compared to the pre-launch forecast. Surprises here often indicate longer-than-expected context windows or more tool calls per session than modeled.
Anonymized user feedback:
a random sample of 30–50 user feedback submissions from the launch window, anonymized and categorized by theme.
Team retrospective survey:
async survey sent 48 hours before the meeting asking each team member: what went well, what didn't, what surprised you specifically about AI behavior, what would you do differently.
Previous retro open items:
a list of action items from the most recent previous retrospective, with their current status (closed, in progress, dropped, or carried over).
AI-Specific Retrospective Categories
Standard retrospective formats ("what went well / what didn't / what to improve") are too generic for AI products. Use these specific categories instead, devoting the bulk of the meeting to them:
Model behavior surprises:

cases where the model output diverged from what the eval suite predicted. Common causes: eval set not diverse enough, production prompts phrased differently from eval prompts, or user context that eval scenarios didn't cover.
Prompt brittleness:

prompts that worked in staging but broke on real user input. Typical patterns: user questions in languages not tested, very short or very long inputs, inputs with special characters, or queries that touch the prompt injection surface.
Tool reliability:

for agentic features, how reliably did the model call tools with correct arguments? Did the model fail to call a tool when it should have? Did tool results confuse the model into taking wrong follow-up actions?
Latency-cost tradeoffs observed in production:

how did actual latency and cost compare to projections? Did the team make prompt decisions (e.g., including fewer examples) during the launch window to manage cost or latency? What was the quality impact?
Evaluation-production gap:
how well did pre-launch eval scores correlate with user satisfaction scores after launch? If they diverged, what does that tell you about gaps in the eval methodology?
Observability gaps:
what information would have helped you diagnose incidents faster? What was missing from the traces, logs, or dashboards during the launch window?
Action Item Taxonomy
Not all action items have the same urgency or scope. Classify each item into one of three tiers so the team and stakeholders understand what's happening when:
Tier
Timeline
Examples
Where tracked
Immediate fix
This sprint
Prompt patch for hallucination pattern, alert threshold fix, hotfix for tool call bug
Sprint board, current sprint
Medium-term improvement
1–3 sprints
Expand eval suite, add tool retry logic, build new monitoring dashboard
Backlog, labeled retro-action
Architectural change
Next quarter / PI
Async tool call architecture, multi-model routing, caching redesign
PI planning as objective
Action Item Template

## Retro Action Item

**ID:** RETRO-2025-04-001  
**Title:** Add multilingual prompt handling for non-English user queries  
**Category:** Prompt Brittleness  
**Tier:** Medium-term improvement  
**DRI:** @eng-alice  
**Target Sprint:** Sprint 22 (2025-05-12)  
**Status:** Open  

### Context
During the launch window, 8% of support tickets came from users in Spanish and
Portuguese who received English-only responses. The system prompt did not instruct
the model to match the user's language. This was not tested in the pre-launch
eval suite.

### Proposed Action
1. Add language detection to the request preprocessing pipeline.
2. Update system prompt to include: "Respond in the same language the user writes in."
3. Add 50 non-English examples to the eval suite (Spanish, Portuguese, French).
4. Measure CSAT for non-English users after the change.

### Success Criteria
- Non-English support ticket rate drops below 1% of total tickets.
- Eval suite passes at ≥ 95% for non-English examples.

### Links
- Supporting ticket: SUP-4892
- Slack thread: #ai-launch-retro (2025-04-08)
How to Run the Retrospective Meeting
The retrospective runs for 90 minutes with a strict timebox. The facilitator (typically the engineering lead, not the PM) keeps the group moving.
Close previous items (10 min):
review each open action item from the last retrospective. Mark closed, in-progress, or dropped. Do not carry over items without a new owner and date.
Timeline walk (20 min):

the facilitator walks through a chronological timeline of the launch period on a shared whiteboard. Each participant adds sticky notes for events they remember. This surfaces the sequence of events and prevents recency bias.
AI-specific category review (40 min):

for each AI-specific category (model behavior surprises, prompt brittleness, tool reliability, latency-cost), spend 8 minutes discussing what happened and why. The facilitator captures action candidates.
Action item drafting (20 min):
convert action candidates into formal action items using the template. Assign a DRI to each item before the meeting ends. Do not leave the meeting with unowned items.
Implementation: Tracking in Linear

# create_retro_action.py — programmatically create Linear issues from retro action items
# Requires: pip install linear-sdk (unofficial) or use the Linear GraphQL API directly

import requests
import json

LINEAR_API_KEY = "lin_api_xxxxxxxxxxxx"  # from Linear settings
TEAM_ID = "TEAM_ID_HERE"

def create_linear_issue(title: str, description: str, dri_name: str,
                         tier: str, target_sprint: str) -> dict:
    """
    Create a Linear issue for a retrospective action item.
    Labels: retro-action, and tier (immediate/medium-term/architectural)
    """
    query = """
    mutation CreateIssue($input: IssueCreateInput!) {
      issueCreate(input: $input) {
        success
        issue {
          id
          identifier
          url
        }
      }
    }
    """
    description_full = f"""
## Retrospective Action Item

**DRI:** {dri_name}
**Tier:** {tier}
**Target Sprint:** {target_sprint}

{description}

---
*Created from launch retrospective*
"""
    variables = {
        "input": {
            "teamId": TEAM_ID,
            "title": f"[Retro] {title}",
            "description": description_full,
            "labelNames": ["retro-action", tier.lower().replace(" ", "-")],
        }
    }
    resp = requests.post(
        "https://api.linear.app/graphql",
        headers={"Authorization": LINEAR_API_KEY, "Content-Type": "application/json"},
        json={"query": query, "variables": variables},
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    data = resp.json()
    issue = data["data"]["issueCreate"]["issue"]
    print(f"Created issue {issue['identifier']}: {issue['url']}")
    return issue

# Example usage after a retro meeting
create_linear_issue(
    title="Add multilingual prompt handling for non-English user queries",
    description="8% of support tickets from non-English users. Add language detection and update system prompt.",
    dri_name="Alice Chen",
    tier="Medium-term",
    target_sprint="Sprint 22",
)
Ownership Model: DRI per Action Item
Every action item must have a single
directly responsible individual (DRI)
— not a team, not a shared responsibility. The DRI is accountable for the item closing on time, not for doing all the work themselves. They can delegate implementation but not accountability.
The DRI must be present in the retro meeting and explicitly accept ownership before the meeting ends.
If nobody accepts DRI, the item is either dropped (and recorded as dropped with a rationale) or escalated to the engineering manager to assign.
The DRI provides a weekly one-line status update in the tracking issue. No lengthy updates required — just: blocked / in progress / done.
Closing the Loop: Follow-up Mechanisms
Sprint reviews:
immediate and medium-term retro action items appear on the sprint board alongside feature work. The sprint review includes a 5-minute segment on retro action item status.
Next retrospective opening:

the first 10 minutes of the next retrospective are dedicated to reviewing the status of previous retro items. This accountability mechanism is the most important follow-up step — without it, retros become vanity exercises.
PI planning:
architectural-tier items from retrospectives become explicit objectives in the next program increment planning session. This ensures they get capacity rather than being perpetually deferred.
Weekly Slack digest:
an automated bot posts a weekly digest of open retro action items to the team Slack channel, showing DRI and days overdue for each open item.
Real-World Example: Retrospective Findings
A team launching an AI writing assistant retrospected one week post-launch. Here is a representative sample of findings by category:

## Launch Retro Findings — AI Writing Assistant v1 (2025-04-08)

### Model Behavior Surprises
- [S1] Model refused to improve emails with informal tone (e.g., "fix my email to my friend").
  Cause: overly strict system prompt interpreted "professional email" as universal.
  Action: Relax tone restriction; add informal-email examples to eval set. DRI: @alice, Sprint 21.

- [S2] Model generated legally sensitive language ("guaranteed results") in sales email template.
  Cause: No legal disclaimer instruction in system prompt.
  Action: Add legal guardrail clause to system prompt. DRI: @bob, THIS SPRINT.

### Prompt Brittleness
- [P1] Prompts with quoted passages > 2,000 words caused context overflow, returning empty responses.
  Cause: No input length guard in the preprocessing layer.
  Action: Add input truncation with user warning at 1,800 tokens. DRI: @carol, Sprint 21.

### Tool Reliability
- [T1] Grammar-check tool call failed 3.2% of requests due to empty `corrections` field.
  Cause: Model sometimes omits required field when no corrections are needed.
  Action: Add field-presence validation and fallback to "no corrections needed" response. DRI: @dave, THIS SPRINT.

### Latency-Cost
- [L1] Actual token spend was 1.8× forecast because users are writing longer drafts than UX research predicted.
  Action: Add token budget cap of 2,000 output tokens per request. Evaluate impact on quality in Sprint 22. DRI: @alice.

### Observability Gap
- [O1] Could not determine which prompt variant was responsible for S2 during incident because A/B variant
  label was not attached to the LLM span.
  Action: Add `prompt_variant_id` attribute to all LLM trace spans. DRI: @eve, Sprint 21.
Common Mistakes
Running the retro more than two weeks post-launch:
memory fades quickly. The first week after launch is when the team's experience is sharpest. Delaying for schedule reasons means key observations are lost.
Using generic retro formats:
the standard "mad/sad/glad" or "starfish" formats do not surface AI-specific failure modes. Use the AI-specific category structure described above.
Unowned action items:
items with no DRI are never resolved. The facilitator must ensure every item leaving the meeting has a named owner who has verbally accepted.
Not closing previous items first:
teams that open new action items without reviewing whether the last retro's items were resolved generate a growing pile of unresolved debt. Always start with a previous-items review.
Missing the latency-cost category:
teams focused on quality and reliability often skip the cost discussion. Latency-cost discoveries from production are frequently the input for the most impactful architectural decisions.
Best Practices
Schedule the retro on the same day as the launch — book it in advance as "Launch Retro: [Feature Name]" so it doesn't get pushed.

Maintain a running "surprises log" throughout the beta period — a Slack thread or Notion page where the team logs model behavior observations informally. This feeds directly into the retro data collection.

Cap the action item list at 10 items per retrospective. More than 10 items means the team will resolve none of them. Force prioritization: if a new item needs to be added, an existing open item must be dropped or closed.

Track action item close rates across retros as a team health metric. A team closing > 80% of retro items per sprint is a high-performing team. A team closing < 40% has a process problem, not just a workload problem.
Performance and Scalability

As the team scales beyond 8 engineers, run two parallel retro tracks: one for model/prompt concerns (ML engineers and data scientists) and one for infrastructure/reliability concerns (SREs and backend engineers). Merge action items in a joint session.
For products with multiple AI features launching in the same window, run a single joint retrospective but segment findings by feature so action items don't get cross-assigned incorrectly.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Meeting facilitation:
Miro or Figjam for the timeline walk; Notion or Confluence for the written record.
Action item tracking:
Linear (retro-action label + DRI custom field) or Jira (retro-action component + assignee). Either works; consistency matters more than tool choice.
Surprises log:
a dedicated Slack channel (#ai-behavior-log or similar) pinned during the beta period. Low friction for team members to add observations without context switching.
Weekly digest:
a simple cron job or GitHub Action that queries Linear/Jira for open retro-action items and posts a digest to Slack every Monday morning.
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
Pros
Cons
Structured AI retro (this approach)
AI-specific categories, DRI model, closure tracking
Requires 90-min meeting and pre-work investment
Generic sprint retro
Low ceremony, familiar format
Misses AI-specific failure categories; model behavior issues get lumped as generic bugs
Async Slack retro only
Fastest, no scheduling overhead
No timeline walk; no forced consensus on priorities; action items get buried in thread
Incident-only review
Tightly scoped
Misses model quality issues and cost surprises that didn't trigger a PagerDuty alert
Security and Reliability
Anonymize user feedback samples before sharing in the retro meeting. User quotes used for analysis should not include names, account IDs, or identifiable context.

If the launch involved a security incident (prompt injection, data leak), run a separate security postmortem in addition to the retro — do not merge the two formats, as they have different audiences and disclosure requirements.
Mental Model
Think of the launch retrospective as the team's
flight data recorder review

after the first real flight. You flew with real passengers, in real weather, and the data recorder captured everything. The retrospective is where you analyze that data, not to assign blame, but to update your checklists, training materials, and maintenance schedules before the next flight. The action items are the updated checklists. The DRI is the mechanic who signs off on each fix. The follow-up mechanism is the inspection before the next departure.
Key Takeaways

AI launch retrospectives need AI-specific categories: model behavior surprises, prompt brittleness, tool reliability, latency-cost tradeoffs, and eval-production gap — not just generic "what went well / what didn't."
Every action item must have a single named DRI who explicitly accepts ownership in the meeting. No DRI means the item will not be resolved.

Classify action items into three tiers: immediate (this sprint), medium-term (1–3 sprints), and architectural (next PI). This makes capacity allocation explicit and prevents architectural items from being deferred indefinitely.

Start every retrospective by reviewing the previous retro's action items. This is the single most important follow-up mechanism — without it, retrospectives become a ceremony that generates no improvement.
Cap the action item list at 10. More than 10 items per retro means the team is identifying problems but not resolving them. Force prioritization.

The surprises log maintained during the beta period is the highest-quality input for the retro. Teams that capture observations informally throughout the launch window run more substantive retrospectives than teams that rely on memory.
